file_name: Principal Life Insurance Company - Broker Dealer Marketing and Servicing Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: 5 . Broker Dealer will use commercially reasonable efforts to train and instruct its Registered Representatives not to make recommendations to an applicant to purchase an Annuity Contract in the absence of reasonable grounds to believe that the purchase is suitable for such applicant, in accordance with the suitability and disclosure requirements of the 1934 Act, FINRA Conduct Rule 2310, and any state insurance law or regulation governing the offer and sale of Annuity Contracts, including any state law or regulation governing sales to the public in general (e.g. consumer protection laws or regulations, unfair trade practices, annuity disclosure regulations, etc.) or to senior citizens, as the same may be amended or interpreted from time to time. Broker Dealer will use commercially reasonable efforts to determine that each transaction is completed with a Registered Representative's report indicating suitability, including any required and necessary customer information, and is subjected to a review process in compliance with FINRA Conduct Rule 3010, as the same may be amended or interpreted from time to time. Each application shall be approved by one of Broker Dealer's registered principals, in accordance with all applicable FINRA rules.
perturbation.changed_text: 5 . Broker Dealer will endeavor to train and instruct its Registered Representatives regarding recommendations to an applicant to purchase an Annuity Contract when there might be grounds to consider that the purchase is suitable for such applicant, potentially in accordance with the suitability and disclosure requirements of the 1934 Act, FINRA Conduct Rule 2310, and any state insurance law or regulation governing the offer and sale of Annuity Contracts, including any state law or regulation governing sales to the public in general (e.g. consumer protection laws or regulations, unfair trade practices, annuity disclosure regulations, etc.) or to senior citizens, as the same may be amended or interpreted from time to time. Broker Dealer will attempt to determine that each transaction is completed with a Registered Representative's report vaguely indicating suitability, possibly including customer information, and is subjected to a review process that may or may not comply with FINRA Conduct Rule 3010, as the same may be amended or interpreted from time to time. Each application will ideally be approved by one of Broker Dealer's registered principals, supposedly in accordance with all applicable FINRA rules.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the obligations of the Broker Dealer regarding suitability requirements. Replacing "commercially reasonable efforts" with "endeavor to" and "attempt to" introduces ambiguity. The phrases "there might be grounds to consider," "potentially in accordance," "vaguely indicating suitability," "possibly including customer information," "may or may not comply with," and "ideally be approved" create uncertainty about the Broker Dealer's commitment to ensuring suitability and compliance with FINRA rules. This contradicts the required due diligence and supervision standards of FINRA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: FINRA Rule 2090 (Know Your Customer) and FINRA Rule 3110 (Supervision)
perturbation.law_explanation: FINRA Rule 2090 requires broker-dealers to use reasonable diligence to know and retain the essential facts concerning every customer and the authority of each person acting on behalf of such customer. FINRA Rule 3110 mandates a supervisory system to ensure compliance with securities laws and regulations. The modifications allow for a weaker, less diligent approach, potentially leading to violations of both rules. The vague language makes it difficult to enforce strict compliance with the 'Know Your Customer' and supervisory obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 3110. Supervision | FINRA.org Skip to main content 3110. Supervision (a) Supervisory System Each member shall establish and maintain a system to supervise the activities of each associated person that is reasonably designed to achieve compliance with applicable securities laws and regulations, and with applicable FINRA rules. Final responsibility for proper supervision shall rest with the member. A member's supervisory system shall provide, at a minimum, for the following: (1) The establishment and maintenance of written procedures as required by this Rule. (2) The designation, where applicable, of an appropriately registered principal(s) with authority to carry out the supervisory responsibilities of the member for each type of business in which it engages for which registration as a broker-dealer is required. (3) The registration and designation as a branch office or an office of supervisory jurisdiction (OSJ) of each location, including the main office, that meets the definitions contained in paragraph (f) of this Rule. (4) The designation of one or more appropriately registered principals in each OSJ and one or more appropriately registered representatives or principals in each non-OSJ branch office with authority to carry out the supervisory responsibilities assigned to that office by the member. (5) The assignment of each registered person to an appropriately registered representative(s) or principal(s) who shall be responsible for supervising that person's activities. (6) The use of reasonable efforts to determine that all supervisory personnel are qualified, either by virtue of experience or training, to carry out their assigned responsibilities. (7) The participation of each registered representative and registered principal, either individually or collectively, no less than annually, in an interview or meeting conducted by persons designated by the member at which compliance matters relevant to the activities of the representative(s) and principal(s) are discu
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities and weakens the obligations of the Broker Dealer, contradicting the supervisory requirements outlined in FINRA Rule 3110. The original text's use of "commercially reasonable efforts" is replaced with weaker language like "endeavor to" and "attempt to," which makes it less likely that the firm will meet the supervisory obligations. FINRA Rule 3110(a) states that "Each member shall establish and maintain a system to supervise the activities of each associated person that is reasonably designed to achieve compliance with applicable securities laws and regulations, and with applicable FINRA rules." The modifications to the contract text weaken this standard, potentially resulting in a failure to maintain a system "reasonably designed to achieve compliance" and hence creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7 . Broker Dealer shall ensure that applications for the purchase of Annuity Contracts are solicited only in the states where Annuity Contracts are qualified for sale, and only in accordance with the terms and conditions of the then current prospectus applicable to Annuity Contracts and will make no representations not included in the prospectus, Statement of Additional Information, or in any authorized supplemental material supplied by Distributor. With regard to Annuity Contracts, Broker Dealer shall not use or permit its producers to use any sales promotion materials or any form of advertising other than that supplied or approved by Distributor. Issuer and Distributor shall provide only approved supplemental material, advertising and sales materials, including illustrations, for Broker Dealer's use.
perturbation.changed_text: 7 . Broker Dealer will attempt to solicit applications for the purchase of Annuity Contracts in what it believes are the states where Annuity Contracts are qualified for sale, and generally in accordance with what it perceives to be the terms and conditions of the generally applicable prospectus to Annuity Contracts and might make some representations that it thinks are included in the prospectus, Statement of Additional Information, or in some supplemental material supplied by Distributor. With regard to Annuity Contracts, Broker Dealer will try not to use or allow its producers to use sales promotion materials or advertising other than that supposedly supplied or approved by Distributor. Issuer and Distributor may provide supplemental material, advertising and sales materials, including illustrations, for Broker Dealer's possible use.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the strict obligation by introducing subjective interpretations and discretionary actions. Phrases like "attempt to solicit in what it believes are the states," "generally in accordance with what it perceives to be the terms and conditions," "might make some representations that it thinks are included," and "try not to use" introduce significant ambiguity. This contradicts the requirement for strict adherence to qualification requirements and approved materials, opening the door to potential misrepresentation and unauthorized sales.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 prohibits the offer or sale of securities unless a registration statement has been filed with the SEC or the securities are exempt. The modifications create ambiguity about whether the broker-dealer is truly ensuring that sales only occur in qualified states and in compliance with the prospectus, increasing the risk of violating this section by offering or selling unregistered securities.

Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 77e - Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 77e - Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly— (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or of the mails to sell such security through the use or medium of any prospectus or otherwise; or (2) to carry or cause to be carried through the mails or in interstate commerce , by any means or instruments of transportation, any such security for the purpose of sale or for delivery after sale. (b) Necessity of prospectus meeting requirements of section 77j of this title It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly— (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or of the mails to carry or transmit any prospectus relating to any security with respect to which a registration statement has been filed under this subchapter, unless such prospectus meets the requirements of section 77j of this title ; or (2) to carry or cause to be carried through the mails or in interstate commerce any such security for the purpose of sale or for delivery after sale, unless accompanied or preceded by a prospectus that meets the requirements of subsection (a) of section 77j of this title . (c) Necessity of filing registration statement It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or of the mails to offer to sell or offer to buy through the use or medium of any prospectus or other
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the broker-dealer's obligations, contradicting Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 (referenced in the Law Explanation). Specifically, the original text requires the broker-dealer to 'ensure' compliance with regulations, while the changed text uses phrases like 'attempt to solicit in what it believes are the states' and 'generally in accordance with what it perceives to be,' creating uncertainty about adherence to legal standards. This increased ambiguity raises the risk of violating 15 U.S. Code § 77e, which prohibits the sale of unregistered securities, as it is less clear whether the broker-dealer is truly ensuring compliance with state qualification requirements.

perturbation.original_text: 14. Broker Dealer agrees to notify Distributor and Issuer immediately of any customer complaints or legal or regulatory inquiries (including, but not limited to, subpoenas) regarding any Annuity Contracts offered or sold pursuant to the Agreement. Broker Dealer shall provide full, prompt cooperation and assistance to Distributor and Issuer in responding to and resolving any such complaints or inquiries.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. Broker Dealer will endeavor to notify Distributor and Issuer of any customer complaints or legal or regulatory inquiries (possibly including subpoenas) regarding any Annuity Contracts offered or sold pursuant to the Agreement when it deems such notification necessary. Broker Dealer will attempt to provide cooperation and assistance to Distributor and Issuer in vaguely responding to such complaints or inquiries.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the duty to report and cooperate. Replacing "agrees to notify immediately" with "will endeavor to notify... when it deems such notification necessary" makes reporting discretionary. The addition of "possibly including subpoenas" makes it unclear whether all legal inquiries must be reported. Changing "full, prompt cooperation and assistance" to "attempt to provide cooperation and assistance in vaguely responding" reduces the standard of cooperation required, making it harder to hold the broker dealer accountable for thorough and timely communication.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 17a-4(e)(4) requires broker-dealers to preserve records of all communications received and copies of all communications sent relating to their business as such, including customer complaints. By making the reporting of customer complaints and cooperation discretionary and vague, the modified text creates an environment where it is easier for the broker-dealer to avoid compliance with this rule and potentially conceal information that regulators would need access to. This impacts the ability to properly oversee their sales of annuities, and address any problematic activity. The added ambiguity could lead to selective record-keeping that does not comply with SEC rules.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.17a-4 Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. This section applies to the following types of entities: A member of a national securities exchange who transacts a business in securities directly with others than members of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer who transacts a business in securities through the medium of a member of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer as that term is defined in § 240.3b-12 , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o ); a security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o -10) that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. Section 240.18a-6 (rather than this section) applies to the following types of entities: A security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. (a) Every member , broker or dealer subject to § 240.17a-3 must preserve for a period of not less than 6 years, the first two years
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the broker-dealer's duty to report customer complaints and cooperate with Distributor and Issuer which contradicts SEC Rule 17a-4(e)(4) requiring broker-dealers to preserve records of communications relating to their business, including customer complaints. By changing "agrees to notify immediately" to "will endeavor to notify... when it deems such notification necessary" reporting becomes discretionary, potentially hindering the ability to oversee annuity sales and address problematic activity. The phrase "possibly including subpoenas" introduces ambiguity, making it unclear whether all legal inquiries must be reported. The shift from "full, prompt cooperation and assistance" to "attempt to provide cooperation and assistance in vaguely responding" reduces the required standard of cooperation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.17a-4, discusses records to be preserved by brokers and dealers subject to § 240.17a-3. This modification creates an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction because it introduces ambiguity, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law. This can result in non-compliance with regulatory requirements, leaving legal obligations open to dispute.
